zones.name: E2E Policy Suite
zones.description: End-to-end tests that enforce architectural contracts, CLI behavior, gateway isolation, and spawn-boundary policies across the full monorepo.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

The zone contains two distinct test categories: policy/guardrail tests (architecture-policy, domain-isolation, integration-coverage-policy) that verify structural invariants, and CLI behavioral tests (cli-*.test.js) that verify command-line contracts — keeping these together is appropriate since they share the e2e-helpers.js harness

The shared e2e-helpers.js module centralizes temp directory lifecycle, CLI path resolution, and process spawn wrappers across 14+ test files, preventing duplication and making the harness easy to extend
verify-build.js is a non-test build verification script colocated with tests — it should be reviewed to ensure it belongs here and is not more naturally a CI script at the root level

Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm this zone is fully self-contained — all e2e test files import only from each other (via e2e-helpers.js) and from node builtins, with no cross-zone dependencies.

The three architectural guardrail tests (architecture-policy, domain-isolation, boundary-check in web package) form an intentionally distributed policy suite — comments in each file cross-reference the others. This pattern keeps enforcement logic close to the tier being enforced while maintaining discoverability.

verify-build.js is a non-test utility script colocated in the e2e directory. If it is run outside of vitest (e.g. directly via node), it belongs alongside CI tooling at the root rather than in the test directory, to avoid confusion about whether it is part of the test suite.

The zone's zero coupling to all production zones is architecturally load-bearing: test infrastructure changes are completely firewalled from production code, eliminating any risk of test-helper code polluting production build graphs.

The cross-reference comments in architecture-policy.test.js (@see domain-isolation.test.js, @see boundary-check.test.ts) reveal that the guardrail suite is intentionally split across three test files in two packages — this distributed-but-cross-referenced pattern is a deliberate tradeoff: enforcement stays close to the tier it guards, but discoverability requires following the @see chain.

The 22-file test zone enforces structural invariants across a 5-package monorepo with no runtime dependency on any production zone (coupling: 0) — this is the only zone in the graph where high file count correlates with zero coupling, confirming the isolation is intentional and not a measurement artifact.

The e2e-policy-suite enforces structural invariants for all other zones but has zero coupling to them — it reads the filesystem at test-time rather than importing production modules. This means the policy tests cannot be broken by production refactors, but they also cannot catch violations introduced inside dist/ artifacts that differ from src/. Any architectural guardrail that requires runtime import graph traversal (rather than source-level grep) falls outside this zone's coverage.

The architectural guardrail suite is split across three files in two packages (architecture-policy.test.js + domain-isolation.test.js in e2e-policy-suite; boundary-check.test.ts in the web package). The split is documented via @see cross-references, but there is no single authoritative entry point that names all three files. A new contributor auditing architectural enforcement must follow the comment chain to discover the full suite — consider adding a guardrails index or a top-level comment in CLAUDE.md listing all three files explicitly.

The zone mixes two test categories with different failure modes: policy/guardrail tests (architecture-policy, domain-isolation) fail only when architectural invariants change, while CLI behavioral tests (cli-*.test.js) fail on any CLI output or flag change. Mixing them inflates flakiness signal — a noisy CLI behavioral failure obscures whether a policy test passed or failed in the same run.

verify-build.js has no vitest exports and is not discovered by the test runner; it is silently excluded from coverage and CI pass/fail gating unless explicitly invoked. Its presence in the e2e directory creates a false impression that it is part of the guarded test suite.

verify-build.js is a standalone node script colocated with vitest test files but not executed by vitest — it is excluded from test coverage and CI gating unless explicitly invoked separately. Scripts not managed by the test runner should live alongside CI tooling (ci.js, root scripts/) to make their execution contract explicit.

Policy/guardrail tests and CLI behavioral tests share the same zone and are reported together in CI output. A failing CLI behavioral test (e.g. changed flag name) produces the same test-failure signal as a failing architectural invariant, making severity triage harder. Separating guardrail tests into a distinct entry (e.g. a separate vitest project or explicit tag) would allow CI to treat policy failures as blocking while CLI behavioral failures are treated as warnings.

The zone contains two distinct file naming conventions with no documented rule: policy/guardrail tests use descriptive hyphenated names (architecture-policy, domain-isolation, integration-coverage-policy) while CLI behavioral tests use a 'cli-' prefix (cli-arg-contracts, cli-ci, cli-config). New contributors adding tests have no signal for which convention to follow, risking drift toward a third ad-hoc pattern.

e2e-helpers.js uses the .js extension while the rest of the monorepo uses .ts — the shared harness file is the only non-TypeScript source file in a TypeScript-first monorepo. It receives no type checking, and any type errors in its API surface are only caught at call sites in .ts files, not at the definition.

verify-build.js is not listed in any vitest exclude pattern — its exclusion from test discovery is implicit (vitest skips files without test exports) rather than explicit. Adding it to vitest.config's exclude array would make the exclusion auditable and prevent a future contributor from accidentally adding a describe() block to it.

Adopt an explicit file naming convention for the zone: use 'policy-' prefix for structural invariant tests (policy-architecture, policy-domain-isolation) and keep 'cli-' prefix for behavioral tests. Document the convention in a comment at the top of e2e-helpers.js so new contributors know which prefix to apply.

Migrate e2e-helpers.js to e2e-helpers.ts to bring it under TypeScript type checking. The file defines the shared harness API consumed by all 14+ test files — type errors in its parameters or return values currently propagate silently until caught by call-site inference.
Add verify-build.js to the vitest.config exclude list to make its non-test status explicit and auditable, preventing accidental inclusion in future test runs if the file gains test-like exports.

The zone's small size (4 files) and focused responsibility (MCP route handling + domain gateway) are signs of good single-responsibility design.

The bidirectional cross-zone imports with web-viewer-application (3 outbound, 2 inbound) should be kept stable; any new import paths added here must flow through the existing gateway rather than bypassing it.

The mcp-route-layer sits exclusively at the top of the web package's three-tier stack — it imports from web-viewer-application (below) but has no imports from web-shared-foundation (two tiers below), confirming it delegates foundation concerns to the application layer rather than reaching past it.

mcp-route-layer has no direct imports from web-shared-foundation, confirming it respects the layer-skip prohibition; all foundation access flows through web-viewer-application as the intermediate tier.

mcp-route-layer cohesion (0.4) is lower than both tiers it connects (web-viewer-application: 0.98, web-shared-foundation: 0.44) — this is the expected signature of a composition-root zone whose purpose is wiring, not encapsulation.

mcp-route-layer currently owns routes-mcp.ts and domain-gateway.ts but not start.ts, the file that actually wires routes-mcp.ts into the Express server. This means the composition-root zone is missing its true entry point, and the wiring file (start.ts) is stranded in the application layer below. The zone would be architecturally complete and correctly named if start.ts were pinned here alongside the handlers it wires.

routes-mcp.ts imports createRexMcpServer from rex-gateway.ts (which is in web-viewer-application). This is a correct downward import — the handler pulls its MCP server factory from the gateway in the application layer. The 3 mcp-route-layer → web-viewer-application crossings documented in zone metrics include this edge; it is not a violation but should be verified to flow exclusively through rex-gateway.ts and domain-gateway.ts rather than any leaf server file.

The composition-root zone (mcp-route-layer) is incomplete: it contains the MCP route handler (routes-mcp.ts) and the sourcevision gateway (domain-gateway.ts) but not the server entry point (start.ts) that wires the handler into Express. As a result, the zone records an inbound import from web-viewer-application (start.ts → routes-mcp.ts), which is reported as an upward crossing and misrepresents the actual dependency direction. start.ts should be pinned to mcp-route-layer to make the zone a correct, complete composition root.

The zone name 'mcp-route-layer' implies full ownership of the MCP layer, but the Rex MCP server factory (rex-gateway.ts) lives in web-viewer-application. The zone owns the HTTP handler and the sourcevision gateway but not the Rex factory the handler depends on — a naming scope mismatch that makes it harder for contributors to locate all MCP-related files.

The two production files in this zone (routes-mcp.ts and domain-gateway.ts) likely have no direct import edge between each other — domain-gateway.ts serves sourcevision while routes-mcp.ts depends on rex-gateway.ts (in web-viewer-application). The zone's cohesion of 0.4 derives from shared test proximity, not a production import relationship. The zone is cohesive by test co-location, not by domain import graph.

mcp-route-layer has no direct imports from web-shared-foundation, meaning the composition root cannot access shared constants without traversing the 387-file application layer. The indirect access chain (mcp-route-layer → web-viewer-application → web-shared-foundation) means the composition root cannot be extracted or independently tested without also migrating foundation access.

Rename the zone to 'web-composition-root' or 'web-server-wiring' to accurately reflect its architectural role (Express route registration and cross-package gateway wiring) rather than a single transport protocol. 'mcp-route-layer' overpromises MCP ownership that the zone does not fully deliver — rex-gateway.ts (the Rex MCP factory) is in web-viewer-application.

The web package's three-tier import stack (mcp-route-layer → web-viewer-application → web-shared-foundation) has no detected layer-skip imports — every cross-zone dependency flows to the immediately adjacent tier, validating the directional layering contract documented in CLAUDE.md.

Two zones (monorepo-root, monorepo-integration-tests) contain misclassified files that inflate file counts and obscure true zone boundaries; both should be dissolved via zone pins following the same pattern used for the previously resolved web-unit and logo residual zones.

The monorepo's cross-zone coupling is entirely localized to the web package (5 bidirectional imports + 7 downward imports). All four other top-level zones (root, hench, .rex, docs) and the e2e-policy-suite have zero coupling to each other. This confirms the four-tier boundary discipline is structurally enforced at the module-community level — architectural drift, if it occurs, will appear first in the web package's internal zone metrics.

The guardrail enforcement surface has a blind spot: architecture-policy.test.js and domain-isolation.test.js enforce source-level import patterns via grep/AST analysis, but neither test validates that dist/ build output matches the source import graph. A build tool misconfiguration (e.g. a bundler inlining cross-package imports) could violate architectural boundaries in production artifacts while all source-level tests continue to pass.

All cross-zone import edges in the monorepo are confined to the web package zones (web-viewer, web-server, web-shared). Every other package (hench, rex, sourcevision, llm-client) and every root zone resolves to an isolated island with zero inter-zone coupling. This concentration means web package zone health is the single most important coupling signal for the entire monorepo.

web-shared-foundation is the only import-graph sink in the web package — its public API (data-files.ts, node-culler.ts) is consumed by 387 files through a single intermediate zone; any deprecation or signature change in these two files should be treated as a high-blast-radius operation requiring a codebase-wide impact assessment.

The web-viewer → web-server upward imports (2 crossings) are the only detected layering violations in the monorepo's zone graph. Per CLAUDE.md, web-server is the composition root that wires viewer routes but does not import from web-viewer at runtime. The upward imports suggest viewer code has a dependency on something currently housed in the server zone — likely shared types or constants that would be better placed in web-shared (the foundation layer) to eliminate the upward coupling without breaking the composition-root pattern.

The monorepo's architectural health monitoring has two structural blind spots: (1) zone coupling metrics are invisible to cross-package dependencies routed through gateway modules, and (2) source-level import policy tests cannot detect boundary violations introduced at build time. Together these blind spots mean that a codebase passing all tests could still have violated its documented architecture in production artifacts.

The web package's internal zone graph (web-server ↔ web-viewer → web-shared) is the only subgraph with active cross-zone coupling in the entire monorepo, and it contains the only detected layering violation (2 upward imports). Resolving these 2 imports by migrating the depended-upon artifacts to web-shared would make the web package's layering contract fully downward-directed with no exceptions — a stronger invariant that could then be enforced by an automated import-direction check in the e2e policy suite.

The remediation backlog for zone misclassifications (web-dashboard viewer files, monorepo-root web files) has been identified across multiple enrichment passes but not yet resolved. Each unresolved misclassification inflates or deflates zone coupling and cohesion scores, reducing the signal value of zone health metrics for ongoing development. Applying the documented zone pins should be treated as a prerequisite for trusting zone health metrics as an architectural drift signal.

The concrete source of the 'web-viewer → web-server: 2 upward imports' is start.ts importing handleMcpRoute from routes-mcp.ts. start.ts is the true server composition root but sits in web-viewer-application due to import-graph clustering. This is a zone placement issue, not an architectural violation in the source code — the import is intentional wiring. The fix is a zone pin (start.ts → mcp-route-layer), not a source refactor.

The web package has two distinct composition-root concerns conflated in different zones: mcp-route-layer owns the MCP handler (routes-mcp.ts) and sourcevision gateway (domain-gateway.ts), while web-viewer-application owns start.ts (the actual server entry point) and rex-gateway.ts (the Rex MCP factory). Consolidating start.ts and rex-gateway.ts into mcp-route-layer would create a true, complete composition-root zone and eliminate all upward zone crossings in one pin operation.

node-culler.ts is the only file in web-shared-foundation with a viewer-specific domain purpose. Removing it from the foundation zone would reduce web-shared-foundation to a 2-file constants module (data-files.ts, index.ts), making it easier to reason about blast radius and eliminate the conceptual mismatch between 'framework-agnostic foundation' and 'viewer DOM algorithm'.

The monorepo's sub-layer enforcement has an asymmetric risk profile: the top of the web stack (mcp-route-layer, 4 files) and bottom (web-shared-foundation, 3 files) are too small for Louvain to detect meaningful cohesion, while the middle (web-viewer-application, 387 files) absorbs most files including the actual composition root (start.ts). This size asymmetry means zone-level health metrics are most useful for detecting cross-zone boundary drift and least useful for detecting internal sub-layer drift within the 387-file zone.

The web-viewer ↔ web-server bidirectional coupling (2 upward + 3 downward imports) constitutes a true zone-level cycle: neither zone can be extracted, rewritten, or independently versioned without modifying the other. This is qualitatively different from a unidirectional layering violation — it collapses two nominally separate zones into a single logical unit with a shared fate boundary.

All five packages except web achieve single-zone or zero-coupling multi-zone architectures. Web is the sole package where internal zone coupling exists. This asymmetry means web package changes carry disproportionate architectural risk compared to any other package — a refactor that would be isolated in hench or rex may cascade across 3–4 web zones due to the bidirectional web-viewer↔web-server coupling.

The 7 web-viewer → web-shared imports represent the largest single directed edge count in the entire monorepo zone graph. web-shared-foundation (3 files) is a high-fan-in sink consumed by 387 files via a single hop. No change frequency or stability SLA is documented for data-files.ts or node-culler.ts despite their disproportionate blast radius — this is a missing abstraction contract, not merely a size observation.

The zone graph has no intermediate abstraction layer between web-server (composition root, 4 files) and web-viewer (387 files). The composition root directly wires a zone that is two orders of magnitude larger, with no interface or facade isolating the wiring surface. A thin interface layer (e.g. a viewer-api.ts facade re-exporting only the symbols consumed by web-server) would reduce the web-server→web-viewer coupling surface from unbounded to explicitly enumerated.

The two upward imports from web-viewer into web-server are the only unresolved layering direction violations in the monorepo, and they block the web package's 4-tier layering contract from being fully enforceable by automated tooling. Until resolved, the e2e policy suite cannot add an import-direction check for the web package without producing false positives from these known violations.

Zone health metrics for web-dashboard (coupling 0.73, cohesion 0.27) and monorepo-root (file count inflated by misclassified web files) remain inaccurate across multiple analysis passes because zone pin corrections have not been applied to the manifest. Stale misclassification metrics reduce trust in zone health as an actionable signal for the team.

packages/web/src/server/start.ts is the Express server entry point that wires all route handlers, yet it lives in web-viewer-application (the application layer) rather than mcp-route-layer (the composition-root zone). This single misplacement creates the only upward zone crossing in the entire monorepo. Pinning start.ts to mcp-route-layer resolves the crossing, makes mcp-route-layer a complete and accurate composition-root zone, and eliminates the misleading 'upward violation' signal in zone health reports — without any source code changes.

The mcp-route-layer zone owns the MCP handler (routes-mcp.ts) but the Rex MCP server factory (rex-gateway.ts) lives in web-viewer-application. The two halves of MCP composition — factory and handler — are split across zones, requiring a cross-zone import from routes-mcp.ts into rex-gateway.ts to assemble them. Evaluating whether rex-gateway.ts belongs in mcp-route-layer alongside routes-mcp.ts would determine if this crossing is a placement artifact or a genuine layering choice.

The web-viewer ↔ web-server zone cycle (bidirectional imports in both directions) means the declared 4-tier layering contract in CLAUDE.md (web-server → web-viewer → viewer-message-pipeline → web-shared) cannot be enforced at the zone level — the two top tiers have collapsed into a mutual dependency. Any import added from web-viewer into web-server is indistinguishable from the existing pattern and will not trigger a zone coupling alert, making the layering contract unenforceable without a dedicated boundary test.

web-shared (3 files: data-files.ts, node-culler.ts) is consumed by 387 files in web-viewer with no documented stability contract, deprecation policy, or interface versioning. At this fan-in ratio, any breaking change to web-shared's public API requires touching a significant fraction of the viewer codebase. The absence of an explicit stability annotation (e.g. @stable, @internal JSDoc tags or a STABILITY.md) means contributors have no signal that web-shared changes require codebase-wide impact assessment before merging.

The monorepo has no zone that owns shared type definitions consumed across the web-server/web-viewer boundary. Types currently housed in web-server that are imported by web-viewer create upward coupling; moving them to web-shared would break the upward import but requires determining which types are truly framework-agnostic. The absence of a dedicated schema/types zone forces shared types to live in whichever zone first needs them, creating coupling by accident rather than by design.

The sourcevision manifest schema does not include a top-level 'pins' key — the manifest only contains schemaVersion, toolVersion, analyzedAt, gitSha, gitBranch, targetPath, modules, children, zoneOutputs, and tokenUsage. All references across multiple enrichment passes to 'applying zone pins' describe an operation that either targets a different config file or requires a manifest schema extension that has not yet been implemented. This is a systemic gap: misclassification remediation has been thoroughly documented but the mechanism to persist remediation decisions does not exist in the current manifest format.

The gateway re-export-only rule is fully enforced by domain-isolation.test.js (confirmed: line 501 generates per-gateway tests asserting no logic beyond re-exports and comments). This is more comprehensive than a lint rule — it validates actual file content. This means the previously identified potential gap (logic accumulation in gateway files) is already closed by the existing test suite, and no additional enforcement layer is needed.

The web package's three zones have an inverted file-count distribution relative to their architectural responsibility: the composition root (mcp-route-layer) has 4 files, the application layer (web-viewer-application) has 387 files, and the foundation (web-shared-foundation) has 3 files. In a healthy layered architecture the foundation typically grows as shared utilities are promoted upward. The inverse here suggests application-layer utilities that belong in the foundation are accumulating in web-viewer-application instead of being promoted.

The only zone in the entire monorepo with both high coupling and low cohesion is mcp-route-layer (cohesion: 0.4, coupling: 0.6). Per the severity guide this combination signals a fragile zone, but in this case the combination is architecturally justified — composition roots are inherently high-coupling, low-cohesion by design. The health tooling has no way to distinguish intentional from accidental high-coupling/low-cohesion zones, producing false positive fragility alerts. A zone-level 'role' annotation (e.g. role: composition-root) in the manifest would allow health checks to suppress these alerts for intentional wiring zones.

Four zone remediation actions have been identified across multiple enrichment passes (pin landing.ts to web-viewer-application, pin start.ts to mcp-route-layer, move rex-gateway.ts to mcp-route-layer, move node-culler.ts to src/viewer/) but none have been applied to the manifest. Each un-applied pin contributes ongoing noise to zone health metrics. Batching these four pin operations into a single manifest update would immediately improve metric accuracy without any source code changes.

The web package has no zone-level enforcement for intra-zone sub-layer boundaries within web-viewer-application. domain-isolation.test.js enforces cross-package gateway rules and boundary-check.test.ts enforces internal web sub-layer rules, but both operate at the file import level — not the zone level. A zone-level boundary assertion (e.g. asserting that no file in web-viewer-application's server/ subdirectory imports from its viewer/ subdirectory) would complement existing file-level tests with a zone-aware enforcement layer that survives file renames and directory restructuring.

The e2e-policy-suite is the only zone in the monorepo that enforces structural contracts on all other zones but has no enforcement on itself — there is no meta-test or CI check that validates the policy suite's own completeness (e.g. that all gateway files are covered by domain-isolation.test.js, or that all tier boundaries have at least one policy assertion). The suite's zero coupling means it cannot self-verify; a policy test could be silently deleted without triggering any other test failure.

The monorepo has no zone that is both high-coupling AND low-cohesion simultaneously — the canonical fragility signal. All high-coupling zones (web-server: 0.4, web-shared: 0.44, mcp-route-layer) have moderate-to-acceptable cohesion, and all low-cohesion zones (.rex: 0, docs: 0, web: 0.27) have zero coupling. The absence of the fragility combination is a structural health signal worth tracking explicitly — the first zone to enter the high-coupling/low-cohesion quadrant is an early warning of architectural decay.

The e2e-policy-suite (22 files, perfect cohesion) and hench (155 files, perfect cohesion) are the two largest perfectly-cohesive zones and share a structural reason: both are closed under a single shared utility module (e2e-helpers.js for the test zone, a store/config layer for hench). Perfect cohesion in large zones is achievable when a single shared-utility hub creates a complete import graph — this pattern could be deliberately applied when designing new large zones.

The sourcevision manifest has no 'pins' or 'zoneOverrides' keys in its current schema, yet multiple analysis passes have prescribed specific zone pin operations to fix misclassifications. Until the manifest schema is extended to support pins and those pins are applied, all zone metric inaccuracies (web-dashboard coupling of 0.73, monorepo-root inflated file count, web-viewer absorbing start.ts) will persist indefinitely regardless of further enrichment passes. The pin mechanism should be implemented and its availability documented in CLAUDE.md.

No zone in the codebase has a machine-readable stability contract or deprecation annotation. High-blast-radius zones (web-shared-foundation consumed by 387 files, rex schema barrel with 7-zone fan-out) lack any @stable or @internal signal. Adding stability annotations to zone entry points would allow tooling to flag breaking changes to high-fan-in zones automatically and give contributors a clear signal before making changes.

The mcp-route-layer zone is the only zone with both high coupling (0.6) and low cohesion (0.4), matching the severity guide's 'fragile zone' risk pattern, but the combination is intentional for a composition-root. Add a 'role: composition-root' annotation to the zone manifest entry so health check tooling can suppress false-positive fragility alerts for intentional wiring zones without suppressing genuine alerts elsewhere.

Four zone pin corrections have been identified across multiple enrichment passes but remain unapplied: (1) pin landing.ts + landing.test.ts to web-viewer-application, (2) pin start.ts to mcp-route-layer, (3) move rex-gateway.ts to mcp-route-layer, (4) move node-culler.ts to src/viewer/. Apply all four as a single manifest update to restore zone health metric accuracy without source code changes.

Introduce a vitest project tag (e.g. @policy) on the three guardrail test files (architecture-policy, domain-isolation, integration-coverage-policy) to allow CI to report policy failures separately from CLI behavioral failures. This enables treating policy failures as blocking and behavioral failures as non-blocking without splitting the zone or duplicating the harness.

Add a CI check that validates the e2e-policy-suite's own completeness: assert that every gateway file (rex-gateway.ts, domain-gateway.ts, external.ts) appears at least once in domain-isolation.test.js's assertion list, and that every documented tier boundary in CLAUDE.md has at least one corresponding test assertion. This closes the meta-test blind spot where a policy test can be silently deleted.

This pattern keeps enforcement logic close to the tier being enforced while maintaining discoverability.

The 10 entry points create an unbounded public API surface with no deprecation path: any symbol exported from any of the 10 entry point files is implicitly public. Consolidating to a single public.ts re-export barrel (matching the convention documented in CLAUDE.md for all 5 packages) would make the API surface explicit, auditable, and safely evolvable without a monorepo-wide grep.

rex-gateway.ts (the Rex MCP server factory) lives in web-viewer-application but is consumed exclusively by mcp-route-layer. Moving rex-gateway.ts to mcp-route-layer would co-locate the gateway with its sole consumer, eliminate one cross-zone import, and make the composition-root zone self-contained — a before/after improvement with no source code changes required, only a zone pin update.
File "packages/hench/tests/e2e/cli-init.test.ts" has 100% of import neighbors in packages/hench/src/schema/ but lives in packages/hench/tests/e2e/ — consider relocating
File "scripts/hench-callgraph-analysis.mjs" is pinned to zone "Monorepo Root" but lives in scripts/ — consider moving to ./ to align physical location with architectural zone
Coupling of 0.56 is moderate but expected for a shared-foundation zone with multiple upstream consumers; the metric reflects correct usage, not structural debt.
The 3-file zone cleanly separates data-file constants (data-files.ts) from tree-pruning logic (node-culler.ts), maintaining single-responsibility within the foundation layer.
web-shared is the only web-package zone with zero framework dependencies; any PR adding a Preact or Express import here should be rejected as an architectural violation.

The architectural guardrail suite is split across three files in two packages (architecture-policy.test.js + domain-isolation.test.js in e2e-policy-suite; boundary-check.test.ts in the web package). The split is documented via @see cross-references, but there is no single authoritative entry point that names all three files. A new contributor auditing architectural enforcement must follow the comment chain to discover the full suite — consider adding a guardrails index or a top-level comment in CLAUDE.md listing all three files explicitly.

hench-agent reports 0 coupling despite having a cross-package gateway (src/prd/rex-gateway.ts) to rex. Zone coupling metrics only capture intra-monorepo-zone edges; cross-package imports through gateways are invisible to the metric. Gateway audits are the correct signal for hench external dependency surface.

mcp-route-layer cohesion (0.4) is lower than both tiers it connects (web-viewer-application: 0.98, web-shared-foundation: 0.44) — this is the expected signature of a composition-root zone whose purpose is wiring, not encapsulation.

monorepo-integration-tests is a residual two-file zone with zero cohesion and zero coupling — identical in structure to the previously dissolved web-unit and logo zones; pinning both files to the e2e zone would consolidate all cross-package test infrastructure under a single well-named zone.

4 of 6 files in monorepo-root are web package files (landing.ts, landing.test.ts, boundary-check.test.ts, build-output-contract.test.ts) — dissolving this misclassification by pinning these files to their correct zones would make monorepo-root a clean orchestration-scripts-only zone.

web-shared-foundation and mcp-route-layer have no import relationship (direct or transitive through shared types), confirming correct layering: the composition-root tier does not bypass the application tier to access foundation utilities.

packages/web/src/landing/landing.ts and packages/web/tests/unit/landing/landing.test.ts are algorithmically placed in monorepo-root but are web viewer UI files; pinning them to web-viewer-application would remove misclassified files from the root zone.

The e2e-policy-suite enforces structural invariants for all other zones but has zero coupling to them — it reads the filesystem at test-time rather than importing production modules. This means the policy tests cannot be broken by production refactors, but they also cannot catch violations introduced inside dist/ artifacts that differ from src/. Any architectural guardrail that requires runtime import graph traversal (rather than source-level grep) falls outside this zone's coverage.

mcp-route-layer has no direct imports from web-shared-foundation, confirming it respects the layer-skip prohibition; all foundation access flows through web-viewer-application as the intermediate tier.

scheduler-startup.test.js exercises the web-server startup path while cross-package-contracts.test.js validates inter-package APIs; placing them in a common residual zone obscures that they test different architectural tiers and should be reviewed separately.

monorepo-root has zero coupling to all other zones, correctly reflecting that orchestration scripts spawn child processes rather than importing domain packages; this invariant should be verified by the architecture-policy e2e test on every PR.

web-shared-foundation is a pure sink in the web package import graph — all 7 cross-zone imports are inbound with zero outbound, making it the only zone whose public API changes carry monorepo-wide ripple risk proportional to the 387-file consumer zone above it.

web-viewer-application is the middle tier in a documented three-layer stack (mcp-route-layer → web-viewer-application → web-shared-foundation); its bidirectional coupling with mcp-route-layer is bounded by this stack position and should not be treated as a layering violation.

Policy/guardrail tests and CLI behavioral tests share the same zone and are reported together in CI output. A failing CLI behavioral test (e.g. changed flag name) produces the same test-failure signal as a failing architectural invariant, making severity triage harder. Separating guardrail tests into a distinct entry (e.g. a separate vitest project or explicit tag) would allow CI to treat policy failures as blocking while CLI behavioral failures are treated as warnings.

verify-build.js is a standalone node script colocated with vitest test files but not executed by vitest — it is excluded from test coverage and CI gating unless explicitly invoked separately. Scripts not managed by the test runner should live alongside CI tooling (ci.js, root scripts/) to make their execution contract explicit.

155 files in a single zone with no sub-zone decomposition means intra-hench coupling regressions (e.g. agent logic taking a direct dependency on store internals) are invisible to zone health metrics until they cause cross-package problems. Decomposing hench into sub-zones (agent-core, prd-bridge, store) would make internal layering violations detectable at the zone-metric level.

The composition-root zone (mcp-route-layer) is incomplete: it contains the MCP route handler (routes-mcp.ts) and the sourcevision gateway (domain-gateway.ts) but not the server entry point (start.ts) that wires the handler into Express. As a result, the zone records an inbound import from web-viewer-application (start.ts → routes-mcp.ts), which is reported as an upward crossing and misrepresents the actual dependency direction. start.ts should be pinned to mcp-route-layer to make the zone a correct, complete composition root.

This 2-file residual zone conflates two files that test different architectural tiers: cross-package-contracts.test.js (inter-package API contracts) and scheduler-startup.test.js (web server operational startup). Dissolving both into the e2e zone would lose this distinction. Pin cross-package-contracts.test.js to a 'monorepo-contract-tests' zone and scheduler-startup.test.js to the e2e zone to preserve tier-level test coverage visibility.

The spawn-only boundary for orchestration scripts (cli.js, web.js, ci.js) is enforced only at the source-file level by architecture-policy.test.js. No test validates that the built or executed artifacts preserve this boundary — a bundler misconfiguration could silently inline domain package code into orchestration scripts without tripping any CI check.

packages/web/src/landing/landing.ts and packages/web/tests/unit/landing/landing.test.ts are Preact page components and their tests, but both are algorithmically grouped into monorepo-root because their import neighbors are sparse. They should be pinned to web-viewer-application. Similarly, packages/web/tests/integration/boundary-check.test.ts and build-output-contract.test.ts are web-package integration tests that should be pinned to their respective test zones, not left in a zone named after the monorepo root.

Viewer source files (elapsed-time.ts, route-state.ts, tick-timer.ts, task-audit.ts) remain grouped with build infrastructure (build.js, dev.js, package.json, tsconfig.json), producing a spurious coupling score of 0.73 that masks the true health of both the viewer and build-infrastructure concerns. Zone pins to web-viewer are the documented fix and have not yet been applied.

node-culler.ts is a viewer-exclusive IntersectionObserver engine (its docblock explicitly states 'Preact integration is handled in the CulledNode component') but lives in web-shared-foundation, a zone intended to be framework-agnostic and shared between server and viewer. Its presence in the foundation zone inflates web-shared-foundation's blast radius to 387 files for a module that is only ever consumed by the viewer layer. Moving it to src/viewer/ would narrow the foundation to data-files.ts (the only truly shared constant) and reduce unnecessary coupling surface.

The boundary-check.test.ts file is the sole enforcement mechanism for the 4 documented internal sub-layer boundaries within web-viewer-application. If a new import path violates a sub-layer rule but is not covered by that test, the violation will accumulate silently — zone metrics will remain green because all violations stay intra-zone. Consider adding explicit import-direction assertions for each sub-layer pair (server→viewer, viewer→messaging, messaging→shared) to make coverage gaps visible.

packages/web/src/server/start.ts is algorithmically placed in web-viewer-application but is the actual server composition root — it imports handleMcpRoute from routes-mcp.ts (mcp-route-layer), creating an upward zone crossing. Pinning start.ts to mcp-route-layer alongside the handler it wires would eliminate the upward import, restore the pure-sink property of the composition-root zone, and make mcp-route-layer's zone membership structurally accurate.
Add verify-build.js to the vitest.config exclude list to make its non-test status explicit and auditable, preventing accidental inclusion in future test runs if the file gains test-like exports.

Adopt an explicit file naming convention for the zone: use 'policy-' prefix for structural invariant tests (policy-architecture, policy-domain-isolation) and keep 'cli-' prefix for behavioral tests. Document the convention in a comment at the top of e2e-helpers.js so new contributors know which prefix to apply.

Migrate e2e-helpers.js to e2e-helpers.ts to bring it under TypeScript type checking. The file defines the shared harness API consumed by all 14+ test files — type errors in its parameters or return values currently propagate silently until caught by call-site inference.

Add a CI check that validates the e2e-policy-suite's own completeness: assert that every gateway file (rex-gateway.ts, domain-gateway.ts, external.ts) appears at least once in domain-isolation.test.js's assertion list, and that every documented tier boundary in CLAUDE.md has at least one corresponding test assertion. This closes the meta-test blind spot where a policy test can be silently deleted.

Four zone pin corrections have been identified across multiple enrichment passes but remain unapplied: (1) pin landing.ts + landing.test.ts to web-viewer-application, (2) pin start.ts to mcp-route-layer, (3) move rex-gateway.ts to mcp-route-layer, (4) move node-culler.ts to src/viewer/. Apply all four as a single manifest update to restore zone health metric accuracy without source code changes.

Introduce a vitest project tag (e.g. @policy) on the three guardrail test files (architecture-policy, domain-isolation, integration-coverage-policy) to allow CI to report policy failures separately from CLI behavioral failures. This enables treating policy failures as blocking and behavioral failures as non-blocking without splitting the zone or duplicating the harness.

No zone in the codebase has a machine-readable stability contract or deprecation annotation. High-blast-radius zones (web-shared-foundation consumed by 387 files, rex schema barrel with 7-zone fan-out) lack any @stable or @internal signal. Adding stability annotations to zone entry points would allow tooling to flag breaking changes to high-fan-in zones automatically and give contributors a clear signal before making changes.

The mcp-route-layer zone is the only zone with both high coupling (0.6) and low cohesion (0.4), matching the severity guide's 'fragile zone' risk pattern, but the combination is intentional for a composition-root. Add a 'role: composition-root' annotation to the zone manifest entry so health check tooling can suppress false-positive fragility alerts for intentional wiring zones without suppressing genuine alerts elsewhere.

The sourcevision manifest has no 'pins' or 'zoneOverrides' keys in its current schema, yet multiple analysis passes have prescribed specific zone pin operations to fix misclassifications. Until the manifest schema is extended to support pins and those pins are applied, all zone metric inaccuracies (web-dashboard coupling of 0.73, monorepo-root inflated file count, web-viewer absorbing start.ts) will persist indefinitely regardless of further enrichment passes. The pin mechanism should be implemented and its availability documented in CLAUDE.md.
